ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for 'beef burrito bowl'. The broader term 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002764) is used as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific term for mixed beef-rice-vegetable bowl dishes is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef meat dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No specific code for burrito bowl or mixed beef-rice bowl exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for beef burrito bowl identified. SNOMED CT does not catalogue composite restaurant-style dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of a beef burrito bowl is approximately 350 g, representing a typical restaurant or takeaway portion comprising seasoned ground beef, cooked rice, vegetables, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Burrito Bowl — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Burrito Bowl (cr_id G00010837), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable beef-rice-vegetable bowl components and are documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White rice (boiled) GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a beef burrito bowl as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being cooked white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with protein (ground beef ~10.9 g/100g) and fat (13.7 g/100g), which attenuate the glycemic response. A blended GI of approximately 56 is defensible for this macronutrient profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 70.2 g for 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 39.3; however, adjusted to 22.5 using available carbohydrate fraction (net carbs ~40.2 g per 350 g serving after fibre deduction: 20.06 − 1.4 = 18.66 g/100g × 3.5 = 65.3 g net carbs per serving; GL = 56 × 40.2 / 100 ≈ 22.5). This is an analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct GI measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 470 mg/100g with low potassium offset (126 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 470 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods; with a 350 g serving delivering approximately 1,645 mg sodium (over 70% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning, omit cheese and sour cream, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (20.1 g/100g) from white rice with medium estimated GI (~56)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated medium GI (~56) and moderate carbohydrate content (20.1 g/100g) require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice will lower the glycemic response, and pairing with the existing protein (10.9 g/100g) and fat (13.7 g/100g) content helps moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 212 mg/100g, estimated saturated fat ~5.2 g/100g, trans fat ~0.1 g/100g, and sodium 470 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high cholesterol (212 mg/100g), elevated saturated fat (~5.2 g/100g), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100g from ground beef), and high sodium (470 mg/100g) presents a high cardiovascular concern; individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request leaner beef, omit cheese and sour cream, and choose a smaller portion of 150–200 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); bell peppers are nightshades; trace trans fat present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground beef is a primary pro-inflammatory food for rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content, which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity; individuals with RA should avoid or substitute beef with chicken or legumes, monitor nightshade tolerance (bell peppers), and request guacamole as the primary fat source for MUFA benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Beef Burrito Bowl is a Tex-Mex inspired assembled dish consisting of seasoned ground beef, cooked white rice, sautéed or fresh bell peppers, onions, salsa, shredded cheese, and sour cream, served in a single bowl without a tortilla wrap. The dish is popular in Singapore as a Western fast-casual meal available at lunch and dinner. The beef is typically seasoned with cumin, chilli powder, garlic, and salt, giving the bowl a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with a hearty texture from the rice and tender beef. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 249 kcal, 10.9 g protein, 13.7 g fat (including approximately 5.2 g saturated fat and trace trans fat from ground beef), and 20.1 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre and 1.75 g sugar. Sodium is elevated at 470 mg/100g, making this a high-sodium dish. Cholesterol is notably high at 212 mg/100g. The estimated glycemic index is medium (~56), moderated by the protein and fat content despite the white rice base. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for hypertension (sodium load), high concern for cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, cholesterol, trans fat, sodium), and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, moderate carbohydrate). It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of ground beef. Modifications such as brown rice substitution, reduced cheese and sour cream, and smaller portions can improve the nutritional profile for health-conscious consumers.

Is beef burrito bowl good for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010837, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable beef-rice-vegetable bowl components, reducing overall confidence for those specific fields to medium; however, the primary macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are HPB-sourced at 0.95 confidence, justifying an overall high confidence rating for the record.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (~5.2 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (~5.1 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (~0.9 g/100g), and trans fat (~0.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable ground beef and dairy components, as the HPB Foodo entry does not report fatty acid fractions. These values should be updated when HPB publishes fatty acid data for this entry. 2. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close USDA SR Legacy composite match was available to provide defensible estimates for all fields. 3. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database) attenuated by the protein and fat content of the dish; this should be updated if a direct GI measurement for a beef burrito bowl becomes available. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish may contain non-halal beef or pork-derived dairy additives depending on preparation. 5. Gluten-free status is not confirmed due to potential gluten in seasoning blends.
